EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere was in place across Sheridan County Wyoming. CAPES were on the order of 2000-3000 J/KG along with a low-level jet around 45 kts. This, combined with a weak mid-level impulse, helped to generate a severe thunderstorms across Sheridan County Wyoming.
A slow moving thunderstorm producing heavy rain resulted in some flash flooding across the western portions of Sheridan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy, slow-moving thunderstorm resulted in flash flooding to portions of western Sheridan County. Highway 14 had water flowing across it at mile marker 84 1/2 resulting in the highway being closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved into the Billings Forecast Area during the late evening hours on the 2nd. Precipitation initially fell as light rain then freezing rain as temperatures dropped during the late evening hours on the 2nd into the early morning hours of the 3rd. The rain then changed to snow and accumulated on already icy road surfaces. Icy road conditions continued for the next week to ten days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 8 inches occurred across the area. Interstate 90 south of Sheridan was closed for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler climbing a steep slope near Corral Creek east of Afton triggered an avalanche that carried him down the mountain and buried him. His companions were able to scramble out of the slide's path and then quickly ride to his rescue. A fellow rider captured the slide and subsequent rescue via a helmet-mounted camera. The total depth of the slide was estimated at two to three feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dramatic video captured a snowmobiler caught in and eventually buried by an avalanche. Companions quickly came to his rescue and uncovered the man.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low south of Wyoming sent waves of moisture northward over central and eastern Wyoming during the Memorial Day holiday weekend. Measured and estimated rainfall totals ranged from two to around five inches. This resulted in flooding and flash flooding in several areas. Johnson County saw significant flooding along the tributaries and main stem of the Powder River and around Buffalo. The greatest impact was felt in Hot Springs County where heavy rains in the Wind River Canyon resulted in several mud and rock slides that closed State Highway 789 between Thermopolis and Shoshoni. The slides also damaged several sections of railroad track in the same area subsequently shutting down rail traffic. Minor flooding was also noted in Fremont County where water approached homes in low lying areas of Lyons Valley near Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding near the town of Thermopolis. South of Thermopolis, Buffalo Creek overflowed its banks and flooded nearby low lying areas. A mobile home community experienced flooding in East Thermopolis as small creeks and streams were inundated with water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was observed 17 miles west of Douglas.

Johnson County saw significant flooding along the tributaries and main stem of the Powder River and around Buffalo. The slides also damaged several sections of railroad track in the same area subsequently shutting down rail traffic. Minor flooding was also noted in Fremont County where water approached homes in low lying areas of Lyons Valley near Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell in southern Hot Springs County. This triggered numerous mud and rock slides, some tumbling several hundred feet or more to the bottom of the Wind River Canyon. State Highway 789 through the canyon was covered with mud, rock, and debris in several locations between mileposts 120 and 123. One Wyoming Department of Transportation employee was briefly trapped in his front-end loader as he tried to clear away small slides at the onset of the event. The massive amount of debris on the highway resulted in the closure of the road from Sunday afternoon, May 24, until Wednesday morning, May 27. Additional slides on the west side of the Wind River Canyon destroyed several sections of railroad track that resulted in a halt of train traffic for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low south of Wyoming sent waves of moisture northward over central and eastern Wyoming during the Memorial Day holiday weekend. Johnson County saw significant flooding along the tributaries and main stem of the Powder River and around Buffalo. The greatest impact was felt in Hot Springs County where heavy rains in the Wind River Canyon resulted in several mud and rock slides that closed State Highway 789 between Thermopolis and Shoshoni. The slides also damaged several sections of railroad track in the same area subsequently shutting down rail traffic. Minor flooding was also noted in Fremont County where water approached homes in low lying areas of Lyons Valley near Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell in the tributaries of the Powder River in southern Johnson and northern Natrona counties. The South Fork of the Powder River along with Salt Creek were two of the major contributors to a rapid rise in the water level of the Powder River near Sussex. High water on both the North and Middle forks of the Powder River also served to flood the main stem. The river climbed more than 10 feet in less than 24 hours at Sussex and crested at 15.63 feet around 0500MST on Monday, May 25. This crest topped the previous record crest of 15.16 feet set in May 1978. Agricultural land downstream of Kaycee near Sussex was completely inundated and several ranches and vehicles were surrounded by water.

However, there was flooding of some infrastructure near the Interstate 90 Powder River bridge in eastern Johnson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very heavy and slow-moving thunderstorm produced flash flooding between Sheridan and Big Horn Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very heavy thunderstorm moved over the Sheridan Foothills. As a result of the storm, Little Goose Creek and Maverick Creek breached their banks with fast moving water. Several roads were completely flooded and washed out. In addition, Bird Farm Road between Big Horn and Banner flooded. There was also a report of a foot of water that rushed into homes 6 miles south of Sheridan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slid south across much of Wyoming as a slow-moving upper level low tracked south of the state. This upper level low wrapped moisture into central Wyoming which combined with the colder air to produce heavy snow across Natrona County and portions of Fremont County. The heaviest snow amounts of 16 inches to nearly two feet fell on Casper Mountain. Snowfall in and around Casper generally ranged from six to ten inches. Six inches of snow was also measured at Jeffrey City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters measured 4 to 10.5 inches of snow around Casper. The highest amounts of snow were in south Casper, near the foothills of Casper Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pacific trough and cold front moved across Wyoming and produced heavy snow in portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell along the east slopes of the Wind River Range where 18 inches of snow was reported at the St. Lawrence SNOTEL. Heavy snow also fell in and around Lander where amounts generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches. Other heavy snowfall amounts included 10 inches at Powder River in Natrona County and 7 inches at Jeffrey City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer measured ten inches of snow at Powder River. Accumulations were less across the remainder of Natrona County, generally ranging from two to five inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and instability ahead of an upper level low allowed for afternoon thunderstorms to develop across portions of central and northern Wyoming. Many of the storms were moving south to north within the counter-clockwise rotation of the low. Heavy rain, small hail, and funnel clouds were all observed in the Wind River and Big Horn basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public witnessed four funnel clouds south of US Highway 20/26 between Shoshoni and Moneta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and instability ahead of an upper level low allowed for afternoon thunderstorms to develop across portions of central and northern Wyoming. Many of the storms were moving south to north within the counter-clockwise rotation of the low. Heavy rain, small hail, and funnel clouds were all observed in the Wind River and Big Horn basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm rapidly developed to the south of Cody and tracked north over the city producing heavy rain and copious amounts of small hail. Hail drifts were measured in feet in some areas with a few low spots accumulating close to six feet of hail. The hail prevented the heavy rain from draining easily and water accumulated on area roads. Several business owners in downtown Cody reported minor flooding as the water piled up in the streets, especially along Sheridan Avenue. A bakery had water on the floors and in the basement and a gift shop flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of one-half to one inch inundated dry washes and arroyos in and around Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site. Water and mud flowing out of one small canyon caused damage to at least six campsites in the middle portion of the site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at the Lander Airport measured 7.6 inches of snow. Amounts around the Lander foothills generally ranged from 3 to 9 inches, with the higher amounts in the high elevations toward Sinks Canyon State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed along a frontal boundary over eastern Park County. There was sufficient wind shear to generate a thunderstorm that produced a rope tornado over open country near US Highway 14/16/20. The tornado caused no damage; however, the associated thunderstorm did produce nickel sized hail that fell on the highway and was several inches deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail up to the size of nickels covering US Highway 14/16/20 near milepost 71.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty northwest wind of 15 to 30 mph in association with passage of a weak cold front stirred to life a controlled burn west of Byron around 1620MST on Saturday, April 4. Approximately 300 acres were consumed as the fire raced east along the bottom land of the Shoshone River and then up rocky terrain. At least one home was evacuated as the flames approached, but firefighters were able to protect several structures. The evacuated home did sustain damage when the heat from the fire melted some of the siding, and a nearby greenhouse was damaged in the same manner. Firefighters were also charged with defending a sensitive high pressure gas line. The fire was large enough that crews from both the BLM and US Forest Service assisted the fight. Flying embers did cause minor injuries to two of the volunteer firefighters.

A tightening pressure gradient associated with an approaching cold front produced strong south-southwest wind across portions of central and southwest Wyoming during the afternoon of Tuesday, April 14. The peak wind at the Casper/Natrona County International Airport was 63 mph at 1333MST. To the southwest, a remote sensor along Interstate 80 in Sweetwater County recorded sustained wind of at least 40 mph during the afternoon. Northwest wind behind the front was also strong with a peak gust of 59 mph at 1935MST at the North Big Horn County Airport near Cowley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind at North Big Horn County Airport near Cowley was clocked at 59 mph at 1935MST. The AWOS at Powell Municipal Airport recorded sustained wind over 40 mph for more than an hour during the late evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of rain in three hours caused flash flooding in extreme northeast Laramie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall from thunderstorms produced up to six inches of rain in three hours, which resulted in flash flooding.  County Road 162 and Highway 216 were closed by high water.  Highway 216 was closed between Albin and Pine Bluffs with a foot of water over the highway.  Several roads in and surrounding Albin were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm developed over the Bighorn Range and stalled across central Johnson County for a few hours on the evening of Friday, April 24. Hail up to penny size accumulated to a depth of several inches, including on Interstate 25 where travel was slowed to a crawl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail fell across a swath of I-25 between mileposts 262 and 267. The hail accumulated to a depth of several inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late spring storm brought areas of heavy snow to northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell over the higher elevations of southern Campbell County and the Bear Lodge Mountains. Snowfall of five to ten inches was reported in these areas, mostly falling during the night and early morning hours.

The greatest impact was felt in Hot Springs County where heavy rains in the Wind River Canyon resulted in several mud and rock slides that closed State Highway 789 between Thermopolis and Shoshoni. The slides also damaged several sections of railroad track in the same area subsequently shutting down rail traffic. Minor flooding was also noted in Fremont County where water approached homes in low lying areas of Lyons Valley near Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of two to three inches fell across northern Johnson County and caused flooding along Clear Creek. The flooding was mainly over low lying fields and caused little to no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt in the Wind River Range produced swollen tributaries that combined to raise the water level on the rain-swollen Wind River. The river gauge on the Wind River at Riverton topped out at 9.12 feet, just slightly above flood stage of 9 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Wind River near Riverton. The river crested to 9.12 feet at 0345MST on Wednesday, June 3. Heavy rain of the previous week and increased snowmelt run-off combined to cause the high water. The flooding only impacted lowlands nearest the river on the south side of Riverton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fire started along the Tongue River Canyon Trail, one mile up the canyon from the trailhead, located on the Amsden Creek Wildlife Habitat Management area around 4 pm MDT on August 2nd. The cause of the blaze remains under investigation but is believed to be human caused. The fire burned approximately 1,700 acres in a mixed conifer forest in rugged terrain which made it complicated for firefighters to battle the blaze. Two helicopters, air tankers, smoke jumpers, three hand crews, county engines and dozers were among the resources on the fire. No structures were lost by the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire started along the Tongue River Canyon Trail, one mile up the canyon from the trailhead, located on the Amsden Creek Wildlife Habitat Management area around 4 pm MDT on August 2nd. The cause of the blaze remains under investigation but is believed to be human caused. The fire burned approximately 1,700 acres in a mixed conifer forest in rugged terrain which made it complicated for firefighters to battle the blaze. Two helicopters, air tankers, smoke jumpers, three hand crews, county engines and dozers were among the resources on the fire. No structures were lost by the fire. For more information, please go to http://inciweb.nwcg.gov/incident/4449.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell in association with a strong thunderstorm that tracked south along the western edge of the Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site. The rain fell on already saturated soils and quickly gathered in dry washes and arroyos west of the site. The result was a flow of red water and mud that followed a dry wash and flowed into the central portion of the park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving showers and thunderstorms dropped heavy rain in a small drainage basin west of Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site. Run-off in the basin fed a dry wash that brought water into the central portion of the park. The dry wash directed water into and around a newly refurbished small cabin that serves as a visitor center. There was minor damage to the park road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures led to melting snow in the higher elevations of the Absaroka Range southwest of Cody. Daily snowmelt led to late evening rises on the South Fork of the Shoshone River near Valley. Flood waters were confined to low-lying areas nearest the river and there was no damage to homes or infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on the South Fork of the Shoshone River near Valley reached or topped flood stage three separate times due to diurnal fluctuations between the evening of June 8 and the morning of June 13. Water levels typically peaked around midnight with a crest of 8.43 feet at 0015MST on Thursday, June 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed eastern Idaho and northwest Wyoming during the afternoon and initiated the development of severe thunderstorms. The thunderstorms generated gusty outflow wind and toppled trees in Teton County and both Grand Teton and Yellowstone national parks. The downed trees caused power outages, smashed vehicles, and stranded motorists on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow wind descended the east slopes of the Teton Range and generated a swath of damage from near Wilson north along the Moose-Wilson Road and through Grand Teton National Park. The damage began near Wilson around 1645MST and spread north until shortly after 1700MST. Downed trees damaged power lines, crushed parked vehicles, made roads impassable, and even stranded park visitors on the Signal Mountain Summit Road. Just under 1000 customers were impacted by power outages due to the downed power lines, including park employee housing at Beaver Creek. In all, over 150 trees were cleared and all park visitors were rescued by 2200MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor along State Highway 120 on Meeteetse Rim recorded a thunderstorm wind gust to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere combined with 30-40 Kts of deep layer shear allowed for moderate instability across Sheridan County Wyoming. This combined with a disturbance aloft resulted in a severe thunderstorm across the area.

Rapid snowmelt in the Wind River Range above Dubois caused tributaries of the Wind River to swell and feed the river with run-off. The river gauge at Dubois briefly climbed above flood stage of 5 feet, cresting at 5.01 feet during the early morning of June 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt in the Wind River Range caused the Wind River near Dubois to rise slightly above flood stage. The river upstream of Dubois crested at 5.01 feet during the morning of Tuesday, June 2. The flooding was confined to lowlands near the river and caused no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface dew points approaching 60F combined with a weak upper level disturbance to generate a lone late evening thunderstorm over northern Johnson County. Storm intensity increased rapidly as the cell moved east off the Bighorn Mountains onto the plains near Buffalo. The storm continued to strengthen over northeast Johnson County producing a swath of one inch diameter or larger hail across 10 miles or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell at a ranch along Tipperary Road east of Buffalo. The hail broke one window at a home on the ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of rain fell across southern Campbell and Weston Counties. Runoff caused flooding, which washed out a few roads in southern Weston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain flooded and washed out several roads. Hay Creek flooded Dewey Road between Highway 85 and the South Dakota border. Lynch Road near the AU7 Ranch was washed out. Spring Creek washed out a section of Spring Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of rain fell across southern Campbell and Weston Counties. Runoff caused flooding, which washed out a few roads in southern Weston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain flooded and washed out several roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, jet streak dynamics, and an upper level disturbance sweeping across Wyoming created the conditions necessary to produce severe thunderstorms in central Wyoming. A moisture-laden atmosphere also set the stage for flash flooding. Johnson County took the brunt of the heavy rainfall and flash flooding on Wednesday evening, June 3. Both the city of Buffalo and Interstate 90 were severely impacted by flood waters. Roads were closed, homes damaged or destroyed, and at least a few public buildings damaged. To the south and west, there were multiple reports of large hail in Fremont and Natrona counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail was observed by a trained spotter near the intersection of US Highway 20/26 and Bucknum Road west of Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe over northwestern Campbell County and dropped quarter sized hail east of Recluse.

Evening and late night thunderstorms produced large hail and very heavy rain over portions of east central and southeast Wyoming.  Flash flooding occurred in southern Niobrara County where four to eight inches of rain was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hen egg size hail was observed 13 miles northeast of Glendo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of agricultural land occurred along the Powder River. The flows were the highest since 1978.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms across much of southeast Wyoming produced widespread large hail as well as some flash flooding from torrential rainfall.  The worst flooding occurred during the predawn hours across southern Niobrara County from Manville to Lusk to Van Tassel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as eight inches of rain fell from slow moving thunderstorms across south and southeast Niobrara County during the predawn hours, which resulted in widespread flash flooding.  Excessive runoff upstream into the Niobrara River and nearby creeks caused them to overflow their banks.  Law enforcement officials evacuated residents of Lusk as well as those living along the Niobrara River downstream to Van Tassel several hours in advance of the flood wave.  The worst damage occurred in Lusk where 20 to 30 homes and 15 to 20 businesses were destroyed.  Flood waters and debris rushed down Main Street in Lusk, swamped four blocks, and shut down the town's water supply.  The US Highway 85 bridge on the north side of Lusk collapsed onto the railroad tracks below.  Flash flooding closed a 20-mile stretch of US Highway 20 from Lusk to the Nebraska border, US Highway 85 from Lusk to the Goshen County line, and a 35-mile stretch of Highway 270 from Manville north to Lance Creek. WYDOT closed traffic into and out of Lusk, except for residents and emergency responders.  Various state agencies, including the Red Cross and Wyoming National Guard, assisted in the flood cleanup efforts.  A local command center was established at the Lusk Fairgrounds with water, food and shelter.  The governor declared a state of emergency for Niobrara County.  Fortunately, there were no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over east central and southeast Wyoming produced large hail, strong winds, heavy rain and a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado touched down in open country and moved southeast.  The tornado dissipated after three minutes on the ground.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over northwestern Weston County and tracked southeastward across the county and eventually into South Dakota. The storm produced large hail along its path.

A strong thunderstorm fired off the east slope of the Wind River Range near Lander. This storm passed through town and then split with the left half headed toward Riverton. The right half of the storm eventually merged with another storm and tracked east across Sand Draw and Beaver Rim. These storms generated widespread reports of penny sized hail, in some cases a few inches deep. The largest stones were around one inch in diameter and fell in Lander at the onset of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and law enforcement reported penny sized hail in Lander. Law enforcement also reported that a few stones were the size of quarters.

The combination of a stalled frontal boundary, jet streak dynamics, and an upper level disturbance sweeping across Wyoming created the conditions necessary to produce severe thunderstorms in central Wyoming. A moisture-laden atmosphere also set the stage for flash flooding. Johnson County took the brunt of the heavy rainfall and flash flooding on Wednesday evening, June 3. Both the city of Buffalo and Interstate 90 were severely impacted by flood waters. Roads were closed, homes damaged or destroyed, and at least a few public buildings damaged. To the south and west, there were multiple reports of large hail in Fremont and Natrona counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of thunderstorms on Wednesday, June 3, brought very heavy rain to Buffalo and the surrounding area. The ASOS at Johnson County Airport measured 0.98 inches of rain in less than 25 minutes as the storm moved north and then stalled over Buffalo. The ASOS recorded a grand total of 1.26 inches in less than one hour. Saturated soils were unable to absorb the rain which quickly ran through the streets of Buffalo and filled area creeks. Five Buffalo homes were declared destroyed, and the senior center and BLM office sustained flood damage. Some reports indicated water four feet deep in low-lying areas. The water was running fast enough to lift and carry the top layer of pavement on a couple of Buffalo streets. A few county roads and some private bridges were washed out due to culvert failures as a result of the raging flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tributaries feeding Salt Creek in southeast Johnson County were out of their banks as a result of an estimated three inches of rainfall on already saturated soils. Additional water upstream near Midwest, where 1.07 inches of rain fell in 30 minutes late Wednesday, June 3, moved downstream and exacerbated the rural flooding. The extreme run-off eventually caused rises of over one foot per hour on the much wider Powder River at Sussex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent stalled frontal boundary and an upper level disturbance led to another day of severe weather and flooding across central Wyoming. Heavy rain fell on saturated soils in Natrona County and produced areas of flash flooding, especially west of Casper. The flooding closed US Highway 20/26 for several hours between Casper and Shoshoni. Other strong thunderstorms erupted to the west and northwest, with reports of nickel to half-dollar sized hail and flash flooding of some campsites at Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site near Hyattville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms brought very heavy rain to areas west of Casper and led to flash flooding in several areas. The greatest impact was felt on US Highway 20/26 which was flooded in several locations and had to be closed between Casper and Shoshoni for several hours. The worst of the flooding was between mileposts 15 and 19 around Emerald Acres subdivision. Other flooding also occurred around Bucknum Road and 10 Mile Road. Portions of Poison Spider Road were also flooded and one home was surrounded by water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported between Federal and Spring Creek ranches.  Highway 109 North near North Table Mountain Loop was covered by six inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Idaho and swept east across Lincoln County. The first storm had visible signs of rotation and eventually dropped golf ball sized hail in Star Valley Ranch. A second storm developed later in the evening and impacted the south end of Star Valley around Afton. There were multiple reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lincoln County Sheriff's Office reported quarter sized hail covering roads in Afton. A trained spotter also reported one inch diameter hail just to the northwest of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary remained near the Continental Divide on Tuesday, June 16. Thunderstorms erupted during the early afternoon as an upper level disturbance passed over the area and combined with the moist, upslope flow north of the boundary. The favorable wind profile enabled one thunderstorm to become a supercell as it moved east-southeast across Hot Springs and northern Fremont counties. The storm produced large hail which damaged car windows in the Wind River Canyon south of Thermopolis. The same storm also spawned a funnel cloud near Lost Cabin. Another storm developed in Natrona County and also produced large hail southwest of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inch diameter hail fell in the Wind River Canyon knocking out windows in vehicles traveling State Highway 789.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Already high water levels on the Wind River at Riverton briefly climbed on the evening of Wednesday, June 17, as high elevation snowmelt eventually made is way down the river. Flooding was very minor as the river gauge at Riverton barely topped flood stage of 9 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge on the Wind River near Riverton briefly crested at 9.10 feet at 2330MST on Wednesday, June 17. No damage was reported as the flooding only impacted bottomland nearest the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary remained near the Continental Divide on Tuesday, June 16. Thunderstorms erupted during the early afternoon as an upper level disturbance passed over the area and combined with the moist, upslope flow north of the boundary. The favorable wind profile enabled one thunderstorm to become a supercell as it moved east-southeast across Hot Springs and northern Fremont counties. The storm produced large hail which damaged car windows in the Wind River Canyon south of Thermopolis. The same storm also spawned a funnel cloud near Lost Cabin. Another storm developed in Natrona County and also produced large hail southwest of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels was reported about five miles north of Lysite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent stalled frontal boundary and an upper level disturbance led to another day of severe weather and flooding across central Wyoming. Heavy rain fell on saturated soils in Natrona County and produced areas of flash flooding, especially west of Casper. The flooding closed US Highway 20/26 for several hours between Casper and Shoshoni. Other strong thunderstorms erupted to the west and northwest, with reports of nickel to half-dollar sized hail and flash flooding of some campsites at Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site near Hyattville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Motorists on US Highway 20/26 were forced to pull over due to nickel sized hail and heavy rain reducing visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent rains combined with mountain snowmelt to produce river flooding along the Wind River near Riverton. The river was above flood stage of 9 feet for several days with a crest of 10.27 feet. The flood was confined to low-lying lands near the river and caused no property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wind River gauge near Riverton was above flood stage for over a week because of run-off from melting snow and recent rainfall. Water levels fluctuated each day with the daily peak occurring during the early to mid morning hours. The river crested at 10.27 feet at 0700MST on Friday, June 12.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain in less than two hours caused flash flooding northwest of Wheatland.  More than six inches of water closed Fairview, Bluffview and Brookie Roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of rain from a stationary thunderstorm resulted in flash flooding across southwest Niobrara County.  Highway 20 from Keeline to Manville was inundated with several feet of water.  A culvert on Highway 20 near Manville was washed out.  Law enforcement officials evacuated residents in Manville as well as along the swollen Niobrara River near Manville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms produced strong winds, funnel cloud, weak tornado, and numerous reports of large hail across portions of east central and southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down in open country just east of Box Elder Creek and was on the ground for two minutes before it lifted.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early evening thunderstorms over Laramie County produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported a half mile south of the Laramie County Fairgrounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain the previous week filled the Big Horn River drainage basin with water. At the same time, recent warm temperatures were melting snow in the Greybull River drainage and additional thunderstorms occurred. The combination of the high water on both rivers led to some minor river flooding near their confluence south of the town of Greybull. Flood waters upstream of the confluence threatened to flow across US Highway 16/20 south of Greybull. Some sandbagging was needed to prevent damage to a few homes. The flooding was relatively minor and limited to lowlands near the Greybull River and caused little to no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Greybull River occurred just south of Greybull near State Highway 789. The flooding occurred at the confluence of the Greybull River and the rain-swollen Big Horn River. Heavy rain of the previous week had filled the Big Horn River and the snowmelt run-off in the Greybull River began to back-up near the confluence. A small levee was reported to have collapsed upstream of the confluence causing further problems. Sandbags were deployed to prevent damage. Most of the flooding was confined to low-lying areas and little damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm first developed around 1400MST over the city of Rock Springs. A moisture-laden atmosphere and weak steering winds aloft allowed for the production of very heavy rainfall in a short period of time. Rainfall totals in the hardest hit areas generally ranged from 1.5 to 2 inches, most of which fell in about 30 minutes before a a second round began around 1500MST. This rain fell on soil already saturated from thunderstorm rains the previous day. The result was flash flooding of downtown businesses, city streets, numerous residential basements, and low-lying areas in the business district.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was witnessed just north of Interstate 80 between Tipton and Red Desert Roads to the west of Wamsutter. The touchdown was very brief and the tornado remained over open country producing no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across southern Campbell County during the night, producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows were cracked from the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across southern Campbell County during the night, producing large hail.
One to two inches of rain fell in less than 30 minutes over the Oil Creek fire burn scar, causing localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of several county roads were underwater northeast of Osage, including Skull Creek Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong wind and large hail caused minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over northern Campbell County and tracked southeastward into Crook County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure crossed western Wyoming during the afternoon of Wednesday, July 15. This trough helped to initiate thunderstorms along a north-south line across Fremont and Sweetwater counties. Large surface temperature-dew point differences favored strong outflow wind from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light residential trailer was blown over and knocked into a car on the north side of Rock Springs. The strong wind also dropped large tree limbs and caused damage to a power pole in downtown Rock Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted within a moist atmosphere in place over Sublette and Sweetwater counties. The result was heavy rain and eventually flash flooding in the city of Rock Springs in Sweetwater County. Rainfall of approximately 0.60 to 0.85 inches fell in and around downtown Rock Springs in about 30 minutes. The result was street flooding and accumulating water in one trailer park on the east side of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock Springs Police Department reported flooding in a trailer park on 9th Street near the South Side Belt Route. Up to 3 feet of water collected in low-lying areas of the park and displaced some residents before the water receded shortly after 2100MST. Several media outlets shared pictures of street flooding in and around downtown Rock Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong upper level low and strong winds aloft moved across western Wyoming on the afternoon of Monday, July 27. In addition, a tight pressure gradient generated high wind both ahead of and just behind the passage of a vigorous cold front. The most severe storm of the day tracked northeast across Big Horn County producing large hail and strong wind. Wind gusts of 60 to 76 mph also occurred with much weaker convection given favorable surface temperature-dew point spreads in combination with the pressure gradient. Ahead of the front, gradient winds were strong enough to topple trees, rip limbs, and cause damage in Riverton where a peak gust of 59mph was registered at 1503MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Burlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked southeast from Montana across far northern Campbell County and produced 60 mph wind gusts in the Weston area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced wind gusts over 60 mph at the Gillette-Campbell County Airport.
An early morning severe thunderstorm produced wind gusts around 60 mph in the Newcastle area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from previous convection helped initiate new thunderstorm development west of the Bighorn Mountains just after sunset. One storm north of Shell strengthened and produced penny sized hail at a rural ranch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of pennies was reported by trained spotters north of Shell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed off the south end of the Absaroka Range and gained strength in the unstable air of the southern Big Horn Basin before crossing the Bighorn Mountains. The storm became severe as it descended the east slopes of the Bighorns and headed east into the higher dew point air across the plains of southern Johnson County. Hail sizes ranged from quarter to golf ball as the storm raked areas from around Kaycee east along State Highway 192.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel sized hail fell just west of Kirby.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported at a pair of rural ranches along Tipperary Road east of Buffalo. The hail caused body damage to some vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains combined with snow melt out of the Big Horn Mountains brought flooding to portions of Sheridan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains combined with snow melt out of the Big Horn Mountains brought flooding to portions of Sheridan. Little Goose Creek reached moderate flooding levels. Two subdivisions south of Sheridan were hit hard. Woodland Hills experienced water, up to about two feet deep, running over the road. Just to the north, water at Woodland Park Village reached about 8 feet at the bridge. Volunteers pulled debris out of water to prevent the bridge from washing out. In Sheridan proper, South Park was almost completely underwater, as was a portion of the field directly south of Brundage. There were also reports of flooding along Big Goose Creek above Kendrick Park. In addition, Mavrakis Pond near 8th in Sheridan flowed over its banks on the east side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from around two inches of rain on already saturated soils caused minor flooding in far northwestern Campbell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding along the Little Powder River covered hay fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary along with moderate to strong buoyancy was situated over the northern high plains. This boundary was associated with a strong pressure rise-fall couplet from west to east across portions of southern Montana and northern Wyoming. This resulted in strong forcing and ascent across the area. This, combined with 30-45 kts of deep shear, resulted in organized/sustained severe thunderstorms.

A frontal boundary along with moderate to strong buoyancy was situated over the northern high plains. This boundary was associated with a strong pressure rise-fall couplet from west to east across portions of southern Montana and northern Wyoming. This resulted in strong forcing and ascent across the area. This, combined with 30-45 kts of deep shear, resulted in organized/sustained severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved over the northwest portion of the Big Horn Mountains during the late afternoon on the 27th. A National Weather Service storm survey concluded that a microburst with winds estimated at 90-100 mph hit the area. Damage from this microburst occurred near Highway 14A near mile marker 81. The approximate damage area was 1/4 mile wide and 2-3 miles in length. Two camper trailers were completely destroyed (tossed and turned) and two others were severely damaged. Numerous large trees, up to one foot in diameter, were snapped. In addition, ping pong to golf ball size hail occurred with this thunderstorm.

A strong cold front pushed south across southeast Wyoming, followed by a potent upper level disturbance.  Snow developed over east central and south central Wyoming.  Heavy snow fell over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges, with lighter snow over the northern Laramie Range and east central Wyoming.  West to northwest winds gusting as high as 60 mph created whiteout conditions across the Arlington and Elk Mountain areas, with Interstate 80 closed between Rawlins and Laramie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cinnabar Park SNOTEL site (elevation 9574 ft) estimated 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed south across southeast Wyoming, followed by a potent upper level disturbance.  Snow developed over east central and south central Wyoming.  Heavy snow fell over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges, with lighter snow over the northern Laramie Range and east central Wyoming.  West to northwest winds gusting as high as 60 mph created whiteout conditions across the Arlington and Elk Mountain areas, with Interstate 80 closed between Rawlins and Laramie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light to moderate snow combined with northwest winds gusting as high as 60 mph created whiteout conditions along Interstate 80 through the Arlington, Halleck Ridge and Elk Mountain areas.  WYDOT web cameras indicated visibilities frequently reduced to less than one quarter mile.  Interstate 80 was closed between Rawlins and Laramie.  Total snowfall measured between two and four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and cold front brought periods of light to moderate snowfall to east central Wyoming.  Strong northwest winds of 35 to 55 mph created considerable blowing snow and poor visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The La Prele Creek SNOTEL site (elevation 8375 ft) estimated seven inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tight gradient between a strong high pressure system over Wyoming and a Alberta clipper low moving across the Dakotas generated very high winds across much of southeast Wyoming.  Wind gusts of 65 to 85 mph occurred through the wind prone areas including Arlington, Vedauwoo and Bordeaux.  The high winds resulted in several semi-truck blowovers on Interstates 25 between the Colorado state line and Wheatland.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at County Road 402 measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 68 mph at 18/1000 MST.

Wind gusts of 65 to 85 mph occurred through the wind prone areas including Arlington, Vedauwoo and Bordeaux.  The high winds resulted in several semi-truck blowovers on Interstates 25 between the Colorado state line and Wheatland.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Wildcat Trail measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 70 mph at 18/0251 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed south across southeast Wyoming, followed by a potent upper level disturbance.  Snow developed over east central and south central Wyoming.  Heavy snow fell over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges, with lighter snow over the northern Laramie Range and east central Wyoming.  West to northwest winds gusting as high as 60 mph created whiteout conditions across the Arlington and Elk Mountain areas, with Interstate 80 closed between Rawlins and Laramie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Webber Springs SNOTEL site (elevation 9250 ft) estimated 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system tracked east across the central Rockies and high plains, while an arctic front surged south through the region.  Snow developed west of the Laramie Range and became moderate to heavy at times over the higher elevations. Periods of light to moderate snow occurred from the Laramie Range east to the Nebraska border.  Strong northerly winds of 40 to 60 mph created periods of very low visibility in blowing snow.  Consequently, Interstate 80 was closed from Rawlins to the Nebraska border.  Snowfall totals ranged from a few inches to as much as a foot over the Snowy Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light to moderate snowfall combined with northwest winds gusting as high as 50 mph produced considerable blowing snow and very low visibility. Interstate 80 was closed from mile marker 353 east to mile marker 386.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of high winds affected much of east central and southeast Wyoming due to a persistent large pressure gradient as well as strong winds aloft.  Gusts of 60 to 80 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor at the Converse County Airport measured gusts of 58 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 76 mph at 07/1153 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance in tandem with moist upslope flow and an arctic cold front produced moderate to heavy snowfall across much portions of east and south central Wyoming.  Gusty north winds of 25 to 35 mph behind the cold front created considerable blowing snow and low visibility.  Snow totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches lower elevations to as much as two feet over the Snowy and Sierra Madre mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to ten inches of snow was reported, heaviest near Rawlins and Sinclair.  Interstate 80 east of Rawlins was closed for several hours due to very low visibility and blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow was reported, heaviest near Muddy Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and deep Pacific moisture combined to bring heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide. The westerly flow favored the Teton Range where the heaviest snow fell. Over three feet of new snow was estimated to have fallen at both the Grand Targhee and Grassy Lake SNOTEL sites. More than a foot of snow also fell in portions of the Salt River and Wyoming ranges, the Absaroka Range, and Yellowstone National Park. Favorable northwest flow aloft also brought over a foot of snow to northern portions of the western Bighorn Range. The heaviest snow in the lower elevations was at Alpine where 16.5 inches of new snow fell. Totals in Jackson Hole approached 10 inches.||East of the Divide, a tight pressure gradient and strong mid-level winds mixing to the surface brought high winds to several locations. The strongest recorded winds were near Clark, where a gust to 113 mph was measured. Measured gusts in the Green and Rattlesnake ranges and on the south side of Casper in Natrona County topped out at 88 mph and 81 mph, respectively. Peak speeds in southern Lincoln County, eastern Sweetwater County, and the Upper Wind River Basin ranged from 60 to 66 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site at Shell Creek estimated 16 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and deep Pacific moisture combined to bring heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide. The westerly flow favored the Teton Range where the heaviest snow fell. Over three feet of new snow was estimated to have fallen at both the Grand Targhee and Grassy Lake SNOTEL sites. More than a foot of snow also fell in portions of the Salt River and Wyoming ranges, the Absaroka Range, and Yellowstone National Park. Favorable northwest flow aloft also brought over a foot of snow to northern portions of the western Bighorn Range. The heaviest snow in the lower elevations was at Alpine where 16.5 inches of new snow fell. Totals in Jackson Hole approached 10 inches.||East of the Divide, a tight pressure gradient and strong mid-level winds mixing to the surface brought high winds to several locations. The strongest recorded winds were near Clark, where a gust to 113 mph was measured. Measured gusts in the Green and Rattlesnake ranges and on the south side of Casper in Natrona County topped out at 88 mph and 81 mph, respectively. Peak speeds in southern Lincoln County, eastern Sweetwater County, and the Upper Wind River Basin ranged from 60 to 66 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell in portions of the Tetons. The highest amounts were recorded at the Grand Targhee and Grassy Lake SNOTEL stations. In addition, 33 inches of new snow was measured on top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and an associated upper level disturbance moved across Wyoming and brought heavy snow to the Teton Range west of the Continental Divide and high winds to areas east of the Divide. Around a foot of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Mountain Resort. Meanwhile, the normally windier locations of Natrona County and those around Clark saw wind gusts past 58 mph. The highest wind gust recorded was 91 mph near Clark in the Cody Foothills. Other peak speeds were generally from 60 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted past 60 mph in the vicinity of Clark. The strongest winds occurred at the sensor 5 miles west-northwest of Clark where there were several wind gusts exceeded 74 mph with a maximum of 91 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and cold front brought periods of light to moderate snowfall to east central Wyoming.  Strong northwest winds of 35 to 55 mph created considerable blowing snow and poor visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Windy Peak SNOTEL site (elevation 7900 ft) estimated six inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system tracked east across the central Rockies and high plains, while an arctic front surged south through the region.  Snow developed west of the Laramie Range and became moderate to heavy at times over the higher elevations. Periods of light to moderate snow occurred from the Laramie Range east to the Nebraska border.  Strong northerly winds of 40 to 60 mph created periods of very low visibility in blowing snow.  Consequently, Interstate 80 was closed from Rawlins to the Nebraska border.  Snowfall totals ranged from a few inches to as much as a foot over the Snowy Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Medicine Bow SNOTEL site (elevation 10510 ft) estimated 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moved quickly east across the central Rockies and plains.  Heavy snow developed over the mountains and high valleys of southeast Wyoming, with lesser amounts across the plains.  Strong and gusty north to northwest winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow, which severely impacted travel along Interstates 25 and 80 throughout southeast Wyoming.  WYDOT closed Interstate 80 from west of Rawlins to Cheyenne for an extended period of time due to near-zero visibility in the Arlington area and several surrounding towns at capacity.  Interstate 25 from Chugwater to Wheatland and US Highway 287 from Laramie to the Colorado border were closed due to winter weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow was reported along Interstate 80 from west of Arlington to Wagonhound.  Strong northwest winds with gusts to 60 mph produced near-zero visibility in blowing and drifting snow.  Interstate 80 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and an associated upper level disturbance moved across Wyoming and brought heavy snow to the Teton Range west of the Continental Divide and high winds to areas east of the Divide. Around a foot of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Mountain Resort. Meanwhile, the normally windier locations of Natrona County and those around Clark saw wind gusts past 58 mph. The highest wind gust recorded was 91 mph near Clark in the Cody Foothills. Other peak speeds were generally from 60 to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system tracked east across the central Rockies and high plains, while an arctic front surged south through the region.  Snow developed west of the Laramie Range and became moderate to heavy at times over the higher elevations. Periods of light to moderate snow occurred from the Laramie Range east to the Nebraska border.  Strong northerly winds of 40 to 60 mph created periods of very low visibility in blowing snow.  Consequently, Interstate 80 was closed from Rawlins to the Nebraska border.  Snowfall totals ranged from a few inches to as much as a foot over the Snowy Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light to moderate snowfall combined with northwest winds gusting as high as 60 mph produced considerable blowing snow and very low visibility. Interstate 80 was closed from the summit to Vedauwoo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low and deep Pacific moisture combined to bring heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide. The westerly flow favored the Teton Range where the heaviest snow fell. Over three feet of new snow was estimated to have fallen at both the Grand Targhee and Grassy Lake SNOTEL sites. More than a foot of snow also fell in portions of the Salt River and Wyoming ranges, the Absaroka Range, and Yellowstone National Park. Favorable northwest flow aloft also brought over a foot of snow to northern portions of the western Bighorn Range. The heaviest snow in the lower elevations was at Alpine where 16.5 inches of new snow fell. Totals in Jackson Hole approached 10 inches.||East of the Divide, a tight pressure gradient and strong mid-level winds mixing to the surface brought high winds to several locations. The strongest recorded winds were near Clark, where a gust to 113 mph was measured. Measured gusts in the Green and Rattlesnake ranges and on the south side of Casper in Natrona County topped out at 88 mph and 81 mph, respectively. Peak speeds in southern Lincoln County, eastern Sweetwater County, and the Upper Wind River Basin ranged from 60 to 66 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 7 inches of new snow at Bondurant.

Sustained west to northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with occasional gusts to 55 mph produced near-blizzard conditions over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges, with considerable blowing snow over the remainder of the area.  Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 29 miles north of Torrington measured six inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient and very strong winds aloft brought an extended period of high winds to portions of northwest and central Wyoming. The strongest winds were in the favored areas of the eastern foothills of the Absaroka Range. The winds were strong enough to topple 13 high voltage transmission power poles west of Meeteetse Thursday night, February 5. The resulting power outage affected about 2,100 people. Muddy ground conditions prevented crews from getting equipment to where the towers fell, so some customers were without power until early Monday, February 9.||The strong winds continued on Friday, February 6. A mesonet anemometer five miles west-northwest of Clark had sustained winds of hurricane force for over an hour with a maximum wind gust of 114 mph. At least one home was damaged by the wind. Other strong wind gusts included 94 mph on Chief Joseph Pass, 82 mph at Fales Rock RAWS in southwest Natrona County, 78 mph at Red Canyon and Grass Creek Divide, and 74 mph in the North Fork Canyon along the east slopes of the Wind River Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to near 100 mph toppled power poles and caused power outages in rural areas around Meeteetse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over central Canada provided for a 24 to 36-hour stretch of unstable northwest flow aloft across Wyoming. This flow pattern favored heavy snow on the west side of the Bighorn Mountains and in the Nowood River Valley from Ten Sleep south to around Big Trails. Snowfall in these areas topped one foot with other amounts of about 8 to 10 inches. Heavy snow of 6 to 8 inches also fell in Lander while the adjacent foothills of the Wind River Mountains received 12 to 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site at Middle Powder River estimated 12 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much colder air aloft following the passage of a cold front produced the season's first snow event across much of southeast Wyoming.  Snow accumulations were variable due to initial melting, with amounts from two to five inches.   Interstate 80 and US Highway 30 were periodically closed between Laramie and Cheyenne during the early morning.  There was one traffic accident due to winter weather conditions which occurred around 2:30 AM local time. US Highway 287 from Laramie to the Colorado border was also closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer four miles southeast of Buford measured 5.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and strong winds aloft mixing to the surface combined to produce high winds across the normally prone areas of western and central Wyoming. The highest wind gust of 82 mph was recorded at the Camp Creek RAWS in southeast Fremont County. Wind gusts of 75 mph were also recorded near Clark in Park County and along Outer Drive on the south side of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts past 58 mph were recorded at a weather station near Clark. A peak gust of 75 mph was measured at 1718MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two Pacific weather systems moved into western Wyoming and brought heavy snow to some mountain locations. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where many of the higher elevation reporting sites were estimated to have received over a foot of snow with a maximum of 20 inches at the Phillips Bench SNOTEL site. Heavy snow also fell in the Salt River and Wyoming ranges with the highest snowfall of 17 inches estimated at the Triple Peak SNOTEL site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site at Phillips Bench estimated 20 inches of new snow. Heavy snow also fell at Grand Targhee where 18 inches fell. The higher elevations of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort also picked up over a foot of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient and very strong winds aloft brought an extended period of high winds to portions of northwest and central Wyoming. The strongest winds were in the favored areas of the eastern foothills of the Absaroka Range. The winds were strong enough to topple 13 high voltage transmission power poles west of Meeteetse Thursday night, February 5. The resulting power outage affected about 2,100 people. Muddy ground conditions prevented crews from getting equipment to where the towers fell, so some customers were without power until early Monday, February 9.||The strong winds continued on Friday, February 6. A mesonet anemometer five miles west-northwest of Clark had sustained winds of hurricane force for over an hour with a maximum wind gust of 114 mph. At least one home was damaged by the wind. Other strong wind gusts included 94 mph on Chief Joseph Pass, 82 mph at Fales Rock RAWS in southwest Natrona County, 78 mph at Red Canyon and Grass Creek Divide, and 74 mph in the North Fork Canyon along the east slopes of the Wind River Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind on Chief Joseph Pass was clocked at 86 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight post-frontal pressure gradient produced high winds across portions of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor at FE Warren Air Force Base measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 66 mph at 11/1358 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tight gradient between a strong high pressure system over Wyoming and a Alberta clipper low moving across the Dakotas generated very high winds across much of southeast Wyoming.  Wind gusts of 65 to 85 mph occurred through the wind prone areas including Arlington, Vedauwoo and Bordeaux.  The high winds resulted in several semi-truck blowovers on Interstates 25 between the Colorado state line and Wheatland.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A UPR sensor east of Lusk measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much colder air aloft following the passage of a cold front produced the season's first snow event across much of southeast Wyoming.  Snow accumulations were variable due to initial melting, with amounts from two to five inches.   Interstate 80 and US Highway 30 were periodically closed between Laramie and Cheyenne during the early morning.  There was one traffic accident due to winter weather conditions which occurred around 2:30 AM local time. US Highway 287 from Laramie to the Colorado border was also closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 17 miles west-northwest of Cheyenne reported 3.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An energetic and moist westerly flow aloft produced a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snowfall over much of east central and southeast Wyoming.  Sustained west to northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with occasional gusts to 55 mph produced near-blizzard conditions over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges, with considerable blowing snow over the remainder of the area.  Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Snake River SNOTEL site (elevation 8915 ft) estimated 16.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moved quickly east across the central Rockies and plains.  Heavy snow developed over the mountains and high valleys of southeast Wyoming, with lesser amounts across the plains.  Strong and gusty north to northwest winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow, which severely impacted travel along Interstates 25 and 80 throughout southeast Wyoming.  WYDOT closed Interstate 80 from west of Rawlins to Cheyenne for an extended period of time due to near-zero visibility in the Arlington area and several surrounding towns at capacity.  Interstate 25 from Chugwater to Wheatland and US Highway 287 from Laramie to the Colorado border were closed due to winter weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow was measured 18 miles south of Rock River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and associated cold front moved rapidly across the Northern Rockies on Saturday March 28th. The system produced widespread severe wind, with peak wind gusts around 60 mph, along with critical fire weather conditions. The severe winds hit at the prime time of the day when temperatures were at record levels and humidities were in the teens. The event was preceded by a late winter and early spring of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures which set up unseasonably dry and dead fuel conditions. The high winds resulted in numerous power outages along with minor home/roof damage and tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 60 mph was recorded at the Sheridan Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over central Canada provided for a 24 to 36-hour stretch of unstable northwest flow aloft across Wyoming. This flow pattern favored heavy snow on the west side of the Bighorn Mountains and in the Nowood River Valley from Ten Sleep south to around Big Trails. Snowfall in these areas topped one foot with other amounts of about 8 to 10 inches. Heavy snow of 6 to 8 inches also fell in Lander while the adjacent foothills of the Wind River Mountains received 12 to 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at the Lander airport measured 7.7 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped south across Wyoming and brought snow and wind to portions of the area. The worst conditions were across northern Johnson County where strong northwest wind gusts topped 40 mph for about 15 hours and peak gusts exceeded 60 mph. The combination of strong wind and snow brought near whiteout conditions and temperatures fell from the upper 20s to the teens. This caused several accidents on interstates 25 and 90 near Buffalo and prompted the closure of these roads on the afternoon of Wednesday, February 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts past 60 mph were recorded at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo. The wind combined with a period of moderate to heavy snow that brought near whiteout conditions and led to several accidents and closures of interstates 25 and 90 for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a stalled low pressure system over Colorado produced moist upslope flow combined with sub-freezing temperatures aloft.  The result was periods of heavy wet snow along with gusty north to northeast winds.  Travel was severely impacted  along Interstate 80 from Cheyenne to Arlington due to the poor visibility in falling snow and snow packed roadways.  Twenty-seven persons were injured in a 70 vehicle pileup on eastbound Interstate 80 about 20 miles west of Cheyenne during the late morning of April 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was estimated at Laramie.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Webber Springs SNOTEL site (elevation 9250 ft) estimated 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Circulation around a stalled low pressure system over Colorado produced moist upslope flow combined with sub-freezing temperatures aloft.  The result was periods of heavy wet snow along with gusty north to northeast winds.  Travel was severely impacted  along Interstate 80 from Cheyenne to Arlington due to the poor visibility in falling snow and snow packed roadways.  Twenty-seven persons were injured in a 70 vehicle pileup on eastbound Interstate 80 about 20 miles west of Cheyenne during the late morning of April 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was measured 17 miles west-northwest of Cheyenne.  Semitrailers traveling east on Interstate 80 were among 70 vehicles involved in three separate pileups that occurred at mile marker 342, about 20 miles west of Cheyenne near the Harriman Road exit.  Heavy snow reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile at the time of the pileups late Thursday morning.  Of the 27 persons injured, six were serious or critical.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightening pressure gradient and eventually the passage of a cold front brought strong winds to northern and central Wyoming. The strongest winds were in the favored areas near Clark, where a few wind gusts were hurricane force in advance of the cold front. Peak speeds ranged from 63 to 84 mph. Gusts in the nearby Absaroka Range reached 78 mph. Southwest flow ahead of the cold front combined with favorable atmospheric conditions to generate a mountain wave in the Lander Foothills Saturday afternoon, March 28. A peak gust to 71 mph was measured at Hunt Field in Lander, damaging at least one metal-sided garage in town. In southern Johnson County, peak speeds reached 62 mph ahead of the cold front. Strong northwest wind struck the northern end of Johnson County in the wake of the cold front, with a 60 mph gust registered at the airport in Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few wind gusts past 58 mph were reported at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo, including a maximum gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed across the Laramie valley during the early morning hours.  The combination of the very low visibility and icy road surface contributed to a deadly multi-vehicle pileup on Interstate 80 about 18 miles west of Laramie.  Multiple injuries and two fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very low visibility in dense fog and icy road surface contributed to a deadly, multi-vehicle pileup on Interstate 80 18 miles west of Laramie at mile marker 292 about 8 am.  A 22,000 gallon tanker was on fire.  Interstate 80 and nearby highways 30 and 287 were closed.  WYDOT posted Speeds Advised at 45 MPH and Black Ice Advisory In Effect on their interstate signs.  As many as 25 motorists were injured and two were killed in the pileup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow spread north across the Interstate 80 corridor and into central Wyoming during the afternoon of Monday, March 2. The snow came down hard in Casper and other sections of Natrona County late in the afternoon, with accumulations quickly piling up. Cold air behind a cold front arrived later in the evening causing road conditions to rapidly deteriorate. The snow became more widespread Monday evening and continued into the morning hours of Tuesday, March 3. The combination of snow and cold caused the closure of Interstate 80 and other state and US highways across southern and central Wyoming. The snow dwindled Tuesday morning and roads were re-opened. Total snowfall was 12 to 18 inches on Casper Mountain, 6 to 9 inches around Casper, Lander, and Ft. Washakie, and 3 to 5 inches at many other locations in central and southern Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lander reported 6 inches of snow at 0500MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms produced large hail, torrential rain and a weak tornado over portions of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF1 tornado touched down four miles east of Yoder near Highway 161 and moved northeast at 30 mph.  Much of the damage was observed along County Road 157 and Highway 92 south of Torrington.  The tornado damaged the roof of a Holiday Inn Express hotel on East Valley Road in Torrington, uprooted trees, destroyed power lines and traffic lights.  A barn was destroyed with sheet metal and wood blown 30 yards away.  The nearby home's shingle roof was damaged.  The tornado lifted 25 minutes later about a mile east of Torrington.  Several livestock were killed, including 20 chickens and four goats.

Travel was severely impacted  along Interstate 80 from Cheyenne to Arlington due to the poor visibility in falling snow and snow packed roadways.  Twenty-seven persons were injured in a 70 vehicle pileup on eastbound Interstate 80 about 20 miles west of Cheyenne during the late morning of April 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Medicine Bow SNOTEL site (elevation 10510 ft) estimated 22.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four skiers were climbing the Sickle Couloir on Mount Moran in Grand Teton National Park on Sunday morning, May 17, when a shallow, wet avalanche broke from above. Three of the four skiers were swept 500 feet downhill over rock and ice. One man was killed on the mountain and another succumbed to his injuries three days later. The injured skier was able to descend the slope on his own. Following the initial call for help around 0830MST, the skiers were attended to by National Park Service Search and Rescue rangers.
An evening thunderstorm produced large hail over southern Goshen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed nine miles northwest of Meriden.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Halleck Ridge measured sustained winds of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of three-quarters of an inch to around one inch fell in about 20-30 minutes within the Buffalo Creek drainage in the foothills of the southern Bighorn Mountains. The heavy rain quickly swelled small creeks and streams, causing Buffalo Creek to go from essentially a dry creek to out of its banks in just minutes. The roaring water impacted a rural ranch and roads, but did not damage any structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West-northwest moving showers and thunderstorms tracked up the south slope of the Bighorn Mountains during the late afternoon of October 2. Heavy rain estimated at 0.75 inch to around 1.00 inch fell in a 20 to 30 minute period in the Buffalo Creek drainage. The water quickly ran off the steep slopes into creeks and streams feeding Buffalo Creek. Eyewitness reports indicated the creek went from a dry creek to well outside its banks in just minutes. The raging waters damaged a county road in several spots, overtopped and damaged a ranch road, and ruined fence line at a rural ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance crossed western and central Wyoming during the morning and afternoon of Sunday, October 11. The combination of strong mid-level winds mixing to the surface and a tight pressure gradient produced high wind in central Wyoming and in the Absaroka Range and adjacent Cody Foothills. The highest wind gusts occurred in the Absarokas where a wind gust of 80 mph was recorded on Chief Joseph Pass. Peak gusts in the lower elevations topped-out between 58 and 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor along Chief Joseph reported several wind gusts past 74 mph including a maximum of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow in the wake of a passing upper-level disturbance along with a breaking mountain wave led to high wind in the Cody Foothills. The highest gusts were recorded to the west of Clark, where gusts of 89 mph and 76 mph were recorded during the evening of October 30. Frequent gusts of 60 mph to 70 mph were also measured over the same 3-hour evening period. Strong winds also reached Cody on Halloween afternoon, when wind gusts of 58 mph were recorded at the sheriff's office.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts past 58 mph were recorded near Clark including peak speeds of 76 and 89 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cole Creek Wildfire spread rapidly in windy, dry, and warm conditions on Sunday, October 11. The fire carried quickly through grassy fuels and caused the evacuation of several neighborhoods in the vicinity of Coal Creek Road northeast of Casper. No human lives were lost, but the wildfire did eventually consume numerous homes, outbuildings, vehicles, pets, and livestock.||The fire began around 1720MST on Saturday, October 10, in a wood chip pile at the Casper Regional Landfill north of Casper. On Sunday, gusty southwest wind steadily increased through the morning hours. Sustained wind of 30 to 40 mph and gusts of 45 to 50 mph were recorded by 0800MST at nearby Casper-Natrona County International Airport. These speeds continued through the day before decreasing after 1500MST. Other nearby mesonet stations measured wind gusts of 58 mph to 65 mph during the morning. The strong wind enabled the fire to spread beyond the boundary of the landfill and move quickly to the northeast. Evacuation orders were issued for the Cole Creek Road, Geary Dome, and Meadow Acres areas by 1300MST Sunday. A total of 190 homes were threatened by the flames. By that evening, ten homes were believed to have been destroyed. At the fire's peak, there were 150 firefighters on-scene.||The fire continued on Monday, October 12, as southwest wind again increased by 0800MST. Although less than Sunday, sustained wind of 20 to 25 mph with gusts around 30 mph enabled the fire to continue its trek northeast. Another two homes were destroyed as the fire had two distinct blow-ups, one around 0900MST and another around 1300MST. In all, close to 10,000 acres had been consumed by Monday evening and approximately 1,350 people displaced.||An evacuation order remained in-place until 1600MST Tuesday, October 13, when residents were allowed to return home. Hot spots were addressed on Wednesday before the fire was declared 100% contained on Friday, October 16. The fire cut a large swath south to Edness K. Wilkins State Park and the North Platte River and to the north through small rural ranches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front, producing very gusty winds, swept across the entire Billings Forecast area on Sunday, October 11th. Some of the strongest wind gusts were around the Billings area where multiple reports of wind gusts around 70 mph were reported. However, strong wind gusts were also observed along the foothills of the Big Horn Mountains. The 70 mph gust measured at Billings Logan International Airport was the strongest recorded in central and eastern Montana, as well as northern Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the Sheridan airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across Campbell County, weakening as they moved to the east. Strong wind gusts and small hail accompanied the storms.
A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across Campbell County, weakening as they moved to the east. Strong wind gusts and small hail accompanied the storms.

A severe thunderstorm erupted over the southern Bighorn Mountains west of Kaycee in the warm, moist air ahead of a cold front dropping south from Montana. The storm increased in intensity as it moved southeast off the Bighorns and tapped more unstable air over southern Johnson and northern Natrona counties.||High winds also plagued both counties and eastern Fremont County in the wake of the cold front. A strong pressure gradient allowed for northerly wind gusting to around 60 mph. The wind was strong enough to damage a storage unit at Alcova in southern Natrona County. A portion of the unit, which was oriented perpendicular to the wind, was torn apart and tossed over 100 feet into the North Platte River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gusted to 59 mph at Johnson County Airport in Buffalo.
