Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Idaho and swept east across Lincoln County. The first storm had visible signs of rotation and eventually dropped golf ball sized hail in Star Valley Ranch. A second storm developed later in the evening and impacted the south end of Star Valley around Afton. There were multiple reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported hail ranging in size from quarters to golf balls at Star Valley Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary draped over the area was the focus for the development of thunderstorms on the afternoon of Thursday, June 4. One storm became quite strong over Hot Springs County where nickel sized hail was reported. Convection redeveloped late in the evening over central Fremont County. Thunderstorms quickly erupted west of Riverton and dropped ping pong ball sized hail near Midvale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hot Springs County Emergency Manager reported nickel sized hail along State Highway 431 just east of State Highway 120 toward Gooseberry Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms across much of southeast Wyoming produced widespread large hail as well as some flash flooding from torrential rainfall.  The worst flooding occurred during the predawn hours across southern Niobrara County from Manville to Lusk to Van Tassel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported four miles west-northwest of Wheatland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A evening thunderstorm over extreme southern Goshen County produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed 10 miles west of Meriden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms over Laramie and Goshen counties of southeast Wyoming produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail was observed three miles southwest of Yoder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked southeastward from Montana across northeastern Wyoming during the evening. Hail to the size of golf balls and strong wind gusts accompanied the storms. Heavy rain produced flash flooding in parts of Crook and Weston Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some tree damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening thunderstorms over southeast Wyoming produced numerous reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported 14 miles northeast of Whitaker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening and late night thunderstorms produced large hail and very heavy rain over portions of east central and southeast Wyoming.  Flash flooding occurred in southern Niobrara County where four to eight inches of rain was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed 17 miles northeast of Lusk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over central Campbell County and tracked southeastward across Weston County, producing hail to the size of quarters.
Late afternoon thunderstorms produced large hail over southern Albany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail was observed three miles southeast of Laramie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms produced strong winds, funnel cloud, weak tornado, and numerous reports of large hail across portions of east central and southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was observed five miles north of Guernsey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary draped over the area was the focus for the development of thunderstorms on the afternoon of Thursday, June 4. One storm became quite strong over Hot Springs County where nickel sized hail was reported. Convection redeveloped late in the evening over central Fremont County. Thunderstorms quickly erupted west of Riverton and dropped ping pong ball sized hail near Midvale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail fell at Midvale. The storm continued to move northeast and impact areas along North Portal Road, which was covered to a depth of three inches with hailstones of various sizes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed along a frontal boundary over eastern Park County. There was sufficient wind shear to generate a thunderstorm that produced a rope tornado over open country near US Highway 14/16/20. The tornado caused no damage; however, the associated thunderstorm did produce nickel sized hail that fell on the highway and was several inches deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Bureau of Land Management law enforcement officer reported a rope tornado in eastern Park County. The tornado remained over open country and caused no damage. The twister was photographed from both the west and east sides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning thunderstorm briefly became severe over central Weston County and produced wind gusts around 60 mph near Osage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed across southwestern Crook County and tracked eastward across the southern part of the county. The storm produced wind gusts over 60 mph along Interstate 90 east of Pine Haven.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved southeastward from southeastern Montana across northeastern Wyoming and eventually into southwestern South Dakota. The storms produced hail and wind gusts to 70 mph. Some wind damage was reported in Weston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trailer houses and a stock tank were blown away, hay bales rolled from fields onto a county road, large limbs broke off trees, and shingles were blown off sheds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm erupted over the southern Bighorn Mountains west of Kaycee in the warm, moist air ahead of a cold front dropping south from Montana. The storm increased in intensity as it moved southeast off the Bighorns and tapped more unstable air over southern Johnson and northern Natrona counties.||High winds also plagued both counties and eastern Fremont County in the wake of the cold front. A strong pressure gradient allowed for northerly wind gusting to around 60 mph. The wind was strong enough to damage a storage unit at Alcova in southern Natrona County. A portion of the unit, which was oriented perpendicular to the wind, was torn apart and tossed over 100 feet into the North Platte River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail was reported at a ranch between Barnum and Kaycee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture combined with several upper level disturbances moving northeast in the post-cold frontal air mass produced periods of moderate to heavy snow across portions of south central and southeast Wyoming.  North to northeast winds gusting to 35 mph created areas of blowing snow and poor visibilities.  Snow totals ranged from two to twelve inches, highest over the Snowy Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported at Douglas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large temperature-dew point spreads and a favorable pressure gradient ahead of an upper-level low set the stage for strong outflow wind from isolated late day thunderstorms. Wind gusts around 60 mph were recorded at both the Lander and Rock Springs-Sweetwater County airports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Hunt Field reported a wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a tight pressure gradient associated with a cold front crossing the area and strong mid-level winds mixing to the surface produced high winds in portions of central Wyoming. Peak gusts of 58 mph and 63 mph were recorded at the Fales Rock RAWS and Hiland, respectively. The strongest winds occurred along State Highway 28 between Lander and Red Canyon. Gusts exceeding 60 mph were recorded near Red Canyon for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS site at Fales Rock recorded a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Crooked Creek Fire erupted around 1245MST on Monday, September 28, and quickly grew to over 300 acres by days end. West-northwest winds increased to 25 to 40 mph during the early afternoon and combined with unseasonably warm temperatures and relative humidity around 12% to support fire growth. Over 100 personnel, four air tankers, and a helicopter attacked the fire, which was located about 15 miles west-northwest of Dubois near Sand Butte south of US Highway 26/287. Structure protection was completed at a ranch camp, but the fire did not advance far enough to threaten the buildings. Forest service roads were closed in the area as crews actively fought the fire. Little growth was observed through the end of September with the fire eventually burning about 359 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crooked Creek Wildfire blew up in the early afternoon of Monday, September 28, and consumed 359 acres by the end of September.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported one inch diameter hail approximately nine miles east of Evansville near the Converse County line.

Strong northwest wind aloft coupled with a favorable atmosphere near mountaintop-level set the stage for another mountain wave near Clark. Very strong winds in excess of hurricane force blew in a narrow band near the foothills of the Absaroka Range during the early morning hours of Sunday, November 1. The strongest gust was 88 mph at 0215MST, with multiple gusts exceeding 70 mph over about a 5-hour period. Other mesonet stations in Clark and along State Highway 120 south of Clark registered peak gusts around 60 mph between about 0400 and 0500MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind prone areas near Clark had several wind gusts past 58 mph. The maximum wind gust was 88 mph to the west of Clark at 0215MST.

Strong southwest flow ahead of a deepening, nearly stationary, upper-level low in the Great Basin generated high wind and boosted temperatures to well above normal. Wind speeds increased rapidly during the late morning and continued into the early afternoon hours. The strongest wind was at the Fales Rock RAWS in rural Natrona County where a gust of 68 mph was registered. Other peak speeds at Lander, Hiland, and across eastern Sweetwater County hovered right around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at Tipton. Other strong wind gusts included 60 mph at Continental Divide and 59 mph at the Rock Springs airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two snowmobilers were buried in an avalanche on Teton Pass around 1230MST on Thursday, December 24. The pair were on a steep, east-facing slope of the Horseshoe Bowl at about 9,500 feet elevation when the hard slab avalanche broke above them. The slide, up to five feet deep, buried both snowmobilers. It took one of them at least three hours to dig himself free after he was buried under three feet of snow. He then came to the rescue of the second person who had suffered a broken leg. The avalanche danger was high due to recent heavy snow of two or more feet in the Teton Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche on Teton Pass buried two snowmobilers under up to five feet of snow. One of the skiers suffered a broken leg.

A strong cold front pushed south into Wyoming during the morning hours of Wednesday, November 18. The pressure gradient and favorable winds aloft combined to produce widespread high winds over most lower elevation locations. Ahead of the front, westerly wind routinely gusted between 60 and 70 mph, especially during the morning and midday hours. Peak winds across northern Wyoming were generally from the northwest and occurred immediately after passage of the cold front. Some of the strongest winds were recorded in northern Johnson County, where peak speeds of 74 mph and 84 mph were observed at the Buffalo airport and a mesonet sensor along I-90 east of Buffalo, respectively. The strongest gusts were registered west of Clark during the early morning with the peak gust being 104 mph around 0100MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong wind gusts occurred in the favored areas near Clark. There were many gusts of hurricane force recorded including a maximum of 104 mph around 0100MST.

The pressure gradient and favorable winds aloft combined to produce widespread high winds over most lower elevation locations. Ahead of the front, westerly wind routinely gusted between 60 and 70 mph, especially during the morning and midday hours. Peak winds across northern Wyoming were generally from the northwest and occurred immediately after passage of the cold front. Some of the strongest winds were recorded in northern Johnson County, where peak speeds of 74 mph and 84 mph were observed at the Buffalo airport and a mesonet sensor along I-90 east of Buffalo, respectively. The strongest gusts were registered west of Clark during the early morning with the peak gust being 104 mph around 0100MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather sensor on Outer Drive in Casper reported several wind gusts above 58 mph including a maximum of 68 mph around 0350MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm moved out of the northern Great Basin and slowed down as it moved through Wyoming. This resulted in a rather prolonged period of snow that hampered Thanksgiving Week travel. Moisture overran a dome of cold air in place across central Wyoming and enhanced snowfall across Fremont and Natrona counties. The heaviest snow fell in the areas favored by north to northeast upslope flow, generally along the east slopes of the Wind River Mountains through the Lander Foothills and east to around Casper. Snow amounts of around one foot were common in the vicinity of Lander with totals of at least 14 in the mountains above town. Other locations with heavy snow included the Wind River Basin where 6 to 10 inches of snow fell and south of Casper where 6 to 8 inches was measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell across Natrona County. The heaviest snow occurred in areas closer to Casper Mountain and southwest of Casper. Amounts of around 6 to 8 inches were common in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong Pacific front blasted across the Billings Forecast Area on December 9th. Strong pressure rises were noted behind the front which brought high winds to the Sheridan Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Sheridan Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front backed in from the northeast and moved westward across the Billings Forecast Area. A deep upslope flow was ushered in behind the front. This upslope flow combined with a strong, unstable disturbance aloft, resulted in heavy snow across the central and western portions of the area, as well as the mountain foothills locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 10 inches was reported about 10 miles south of Sheridan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance and associated strong mid and upper-level winds moved across Wyoming and produced high winds in northwest and central portions of the state. The highest winds were in the Absaroka Range where winds were sustained as high as 73 mph. Gusts on Chief Joseph Pass roared past hurricane force with a maximum of 96 mph. In addition, mountain wave activity produced high winds near Clark and Lander, where maximum gusts of 86 mph and 59 mph were recorded, respectively. Across central Wyoming, a tight pressure gradient produced high winds in Natrona County. A peak gust of 70 mph was registered along Outer Drive on the south side of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor 5 miles west-northwest of Clark recorded wind gusts of 81 and 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to northwest mid-level winds spread across Wyoming and mainly impacted northern and central sections of the state on Wednesday, December 9. A tight surface pressure gradient ahead of an approaching cold front and strong winds mixing to the surface behind the cold front also resulted in high wind. The strongest winds were in the favored areas of the Absaroka Range and in the lee of that range. Hurricane force wind gusts were recorded at all the wind sensors near Clark, including a maximum of 103 mph during the morning of December 9. A sensor on nearby Chief Joseph Pass registered a gust of 96 mph around the same time. Strong winds also occurred in portions of central Wyoming. Some of the strongest wind gusts were 70 mph on the south side of Casper, 68 mph at Lander, and a 64 mph gust at Riverton Regional Airport. Strong wind south of Lander blew over one semitrailer and pushed another off State Highway 789 around 1630MST. Both vehicles were empty.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor along the Chief Joseph Highway recorded sustained winds of 60 mph with many gusts of hurricane force. The maximum wind gust was 96 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong feed of moisture from the Pacific Ocean combined with favorable upper-level dynamics and low-level upslope flow to produce heavy snow across portions of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in Natrona County with Casper Mountain receiving the most snow with as much of 32 inches reported. The city of Casper had 10 to 16 inches with higher amounts in the southern sections of town. Thermopolis and Lander also had favorable upslope flow and averaged around 10 and 8 inches of new snow, respectively. Less snow fell in other areas, but gusty winds combined with the snow to produce near white-out conditions that closed major roads including Interstate 25 and US 20/26. Across the west, the most snow fell in the vicinity of Jackson Hole with reports of 6 to 12 inches fairly common, especially north of the town of Jackson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout much of Natrona County with the heaviest amounts closer to Casper Mountain. Amounts where generally 10 to 16 inches across the city of Casper with 9.5 inches at the Casper Airport. Lesser amounts fell in northern portions of the county with 5 to 6 inches at Midwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Terrain-induced heavy snowfall occurred across western Wyoming as a favorable moist, westerly flow aloft enveloped areas west of the Continental Divide. The heaviest snow fell in Teton County with frequent reports of 12 to 18 inches in Jackson Hole and in the Teton Range. Snow totals of 6 to 10 inches were common across the lower elevations of Lincoln County. The nearby Salt River and Wyoming ranges received about one foot of snow. To the east of the Divide, high wind blew in Natrona County where speeds reached 74 mph on the south side of Casper and 61 mph at the city's international airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell across the Tetons from a long lived winter storm. Some areas such as Grand Targhee Ski Resort and Jackson hole received around 3 feet of new snow.

A winter storm tracked across the central and northern plains, bringing heavy snow to portions of northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across northern Campbell and Crook Counties, where six to 12 inches was reported. Gusty northwest winds behind the system produced some blowing and drifting snow on the plains.

A strong feed of moisture from the Pacific Ocean combined with favorable upper-level dynamics and low-level upslope flow to produce heavy snow across portions of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in Natrona County with Casper Mountain receiving the most snow with as much of 32 inches reported. The city of Casper had 10 to 16 inches with higher amounts in the southern sections of town. Thermopolis and Lander also had favorable upslope flow and averaged around 10 and 8 inches of new snow, respectively. Less snow fell in other areas, but gusty winds combined with the snow to produce near white-out conditions that closed major roads including Interstate 25 and US 20/26. Across the west, the most snow fell in the vicinity of Jackson Hole with reports of 6 to 12 inches fairly common, especially north of the town of Jackson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported from 6 to 12 inches of new snow across the Jackson Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Terrain-induced heavy snowfall occurred across western Wyoming as a favorable moist, westerly flow aloft enveloped areas west of the Continental Divide. The heaviest snow fell in Teton County with frequent reports of 12 to 18 inches in Jackson Hole and in the Teton Range. Snow totals of 6 to 10 inches were common across the lower elevations of Lincoln County. The nearby Salt River and Wyoming ranges received about one foot of snow. To the east of the Divide, high wind blew in Natrona County where speeds reached 74 mph on the south side of Casper and 61 mph at the city's international airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of up to around 8 inches fell in Star Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of very cold air over the Yellowstone Plateau and an increasing pressure gradient associated with a southeast-moving Alberta Clipper produced strong winds in localized sections of Park, Natrona, and Fremont counties. Strong gradient wind over 70 mph was measured in extreme southeast Fremont County and in Natrona County. The Camp Creek RAWS recorded a peak gust of 72 mph before sunrise on December 17, while a mesonet sensor registered a peak gust of 71 mph along Outer Drive in south Casper shortly after 0500MST the same day. Occasional strong gap winds near Clark produced a peak wind gust of 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew across favored areas of the Green and Rattlesnake Range. The strongest wind gusts were 72 mph at Camp Creek and 69 mph at Fales Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tight gradient between a strong high pressure system over Wyoming and a Alberta clipper low moving across the Dakotas generated very high winds across much of southeast Wyoming.  Wind gusts of 65 to 85 mph occurred through the wind prone areas including Arlington, Vedauwoo and Bordeaux.  The high winds resulted in several semi-truck blowovers on Interstates 25 between the Colorado state line and Wheatland.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Cooper Cove measured wind gusts of 58 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 80 mph at 18/0150 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance and associated strong mid and upper-level winds moved across Wyoming and produced high winds in northwest and central portions of the state. The highest winds were in the Absaroka Range where winds were sustained as high as 73 mph. Gusts on Chief Joseph Pass roared past hurricane force with a maximum of 96 mph. In addition, mountain wave activity produced high winds near Clark and Lander, where maximum gusts of 86 mph and 59 mph were recorded, respectively. Across central Wyoming, a tight pressure gradient produced high winds in Natrona County. A peak gust of 70 mph was registered along Outer Drive on the south side of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS site at Fales Rock recorded several wind gusts over 58 mph including a maximum of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific low pressure system moved slowly across the Central Rockies and produced moderate to heavy snow over portions of Southeast Wyoming.  Gusty east winds to southeast winds of 25 to 35 mph created blowing snow and poor visibility.  Total snow accumulations varied from 6 to 18 inches, heaviest over the Snowy and Sierra Madre mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whiskey Park SNOTEL site (elevation 8950 ft) estimated 18 inches of snow.
